Summary: Security Issue: October 2018 security updates for R2013b ¨C R2018a MATLAB, Polyspace, and MATLAB Compiler and Runtime
Description: Important security updates are available for R2013b¨CR2018a.   If you are using R2018b, no action is required. This release contains cumulative security updates.   If you are using an older release,?upgrade to R2018b .   If you need to use older releases, download updates linked in the table below.  MATLAB and Polyspace  R2013b¨CR2018a have updates available.             Release       Products       Solution                     R2018b       All updates included       No action required                R2018a       MATLAB and Polyspace       Download R2018a Update 6                 R2017b       MATLAB and Polyspace       Download R2017b Update 9                 R2017a       MATLAB and Polyspace       Download R2017a Update 3                 R2016b       MATLAB and Polyspace       Download R2016b Update 6                 R2016a       MATLAB and Polyspace       Download R2016a Update 7                 R2015b       Polyspace       Download R2015b Update 1                 R2015a SP1       Polyspace       Download R2015a SP1 Update 3                 R2013b¨CR2015a       Polyspace       See Bug Report ID 1843737           For more information about these updates, see:   MATLAB Bug Report ID 1830808    Polyspace Bug Report ID 1843737   MATLAB Compiler and Runtime  Security updates are available for MATLAB apps (.mlapp) deployed using MATLAB Runtime R2016a¨CR2017a. To determine if updates apply to your app, including apps you have deployed to third parties, see the MATLAB Compiler Bug Report ID 1830810 .             MATLAB Runtime Release       Solution                     R2017b¨CR2018b       No action required                R2017a       Download the MATLAB Runtime Update                 R2016b       Download the MATLAB Runtime Update                 R2016a       Download the MATLAB Runtime Update           Ignore the Exists in  table at the end of this report. Version and product information is documented above.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2015b Update 1, R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2017b Update 9, R2018a Update 6, R2018b

Summary: Security Issue: Running Polyspace or MATLAB with Polyspace installed for R2013b ¨C R2018a may allow remote code execution
Description: Security updates are available for R2013b¨CR2018a. Running MATLAB with Polyspace installed may allow remote code execution.   If you are using R2018b, no action is required. This release is not affected.   If you are using an older release,?upgrade to R2018b .   If you need to use older releases, download security updates linked in the table below.              Release       Solution                     R2018b       No action required                R2018a       Download R2018a Update 6                 R2017b       Download R2017b Update 9                 R2017a       Download R2017a Update 3                 R2016b       Download R2016b Update 6                 R2016a       Download R2016a Update 7                 R2015b       Download R2015b Update 1                 R2015a SP1       Download R2015a SP1 Update 3                 R2013b¨CR2015a       See the Workaround  section below
Workaround: For R2013b¨CR2015a, download the attachment and follow the instructions below.Installation instructions  Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.  Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps. On Mac systems, the folder name will have a .app extension, e.g. /Applications/MATLAB_R20XXa.app  Exit MATLAB.  Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Windows Systems: You may need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To do this, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode, then from the browser save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Mac Systems: If you are using Safari on a Mac, disable automatic unzipping of the downloaded file by unchecking Open Safe Files After Downloading in Preferences > General.   For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file:          Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.      Linux/Mac Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Mac):        unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>On a Mac, include the .app extension in the folder name.         Extract the files from the .zip file into the <MATLAB> folder.          Windows Systems:                  Open the .zip file.          Extract the contents of the file into your <MATLAB> folder. You may be prompted to provide Administrator privileges when extracting the .zip file.                    Linux/Mac Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Mac):         cd <MATLAB>        unzip <.zip file>On a Mac, include the .app extension in the folder name.        Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2015b Update 1, R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2017b Update 9, R2018a Update 6, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Using Discrete State-Space block with state name defined as xnew can lead to incorrect answer during code generation
Description: If a model has Discrete State-Space block, incorrect code is generated when all these conditions are met: State name  is xnew .?The name xnew  conflicts with the TLC keyword. Code Generation storage class  is ExportedGlobal , ImportedExtern  or any Custom storage class.
Workaround: Change the State name.
Products: MATLAB Coder, Embedded Coder, Simulink Coder
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mismatch in behavior with MATLAB when using element-wise multiplication (.*)?that mixes?double and int64 or uint64 inputs
Description: The generated code can produce a wrong answer if your MATLAB code contains an element-wise multiplication (.*) ?that mixes double  and int64  or uint64  inputs. This issue occurs?when all of the following conditions are true:? One input is double  and the other is?either int64  or uint64.  The double  input has absolute value less than 2^-12.   This issue affects MATLAB Coder, MATLAB Function blocks and MATLAB System blocks in Simulink, and MATLAB functions in Stateflow. See also Bug Report ID 1849460
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Write to variable missing when variable is size vector argument of reshape function call within a loop
Description: The code generator might incorrectly omit writes to a variable when all of the following conditions occur:The variable is the size vector argument of a reshape  function call. The reshape  function call exists within a loop. There are writes to every element of the variable within the same loop  When this bug occurs, writes to the variable preceding the loop might be incorrectly missing in the generated code. For an example, see the function issue_example_1793805 below.function Y = issue_example_1793805(X,initSize,finalSize)    assert(isa(X,'double'));    assert(all(size(X) == [10,10,10]))    assert(isa(initSize,'int32'));    assert(all(size(initSize) == [1,3]));    assert(isa(finalSize,'int32'));    assert(all(size(finalSize) == [1,3]));    initSize(2) = initSize(2)*initSize(3);    initSize(3) = int32(1);    Y = zeros(numel(initSize),1);    for k = 1:numel(initSize)        if(k == 1 || k == numel(initSize))            Y(k) = locProcess(int32(reshape(X,initSize)));        end         initSize(k) = finalSize(4 - k);    endendfunction out = locProcess(in)    coder.inline('never');    out = size(in,2)*size(in,3) + size(in,1) * sum(in(:));end  The generated code for this function is as follows:void issue_example_1793805(const double X[1000], int initSize[3], const int? finalSize[3], double Y[3]){? int i;? emxArray_int32_T *b_X;? int i0;? int b_initSize[3];? int loop_ub;? double d0;? int i1;? (void)initSize;? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {??? Y[i] = 0.0;? }? emxInit_int32_T(&b_X, 3);? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ?? [SNIP]   The writes corresponding to the statements 'initSize(2) = initSize(2)*initSize(3)'  and 'initSize(3) = int32(1);'? are not in the generated code. These writes are necessary for the behavior of the generated code to match MATLAB. Instead the code generator should produce this code. Note the assignments 'initSize[1] = (int)i0;' and ' initSize[2] = 1;'. void issue_example_1793805(const double X[1000], int initSize[3], const int? finalSize[3], double Y[3]){? long i0;? int i;? emxArray_int32_T *b_X;? int i1;? int loop_ub;? double d0;? int i2;? i0 = (long)initSize[1] * initSize[2];? if (i0 > 2147483647L) {??? i0 = 2147483647L;? } else {??? if (i0 < -2147483648L) {????? i0 = -2147483648L;??? }? }? initSize[1] = (int)i0;? initSize[2] = 1;? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {??? Y[i] = 0.0;? }? emxInit_int32_T(&b_X, 3); ? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? [SNIP]    This issue can happen for these code generation workflows:MATLAB Coder only workflow Simulink code generation of a model containing a MATLAB Function Block, Matlab System Block, or Stateflow Chart
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: The simulation of Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block outputs wrong result for some 1-D tables
Description: The simulation?of Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block outputs wrong?results when all of these conditions are met: Table is from input port.  Table is a 1-D vector with 2-D dimension (for example, N  x 1 or 1 x N ), but is not interpreted as a 1-D vector. The index port input value is greater than the table dimension size. The Inputs select this object from table  parameter of the block is set as Element .  In R2013b and earlier releases, both simulation and code generation output wrong results?for these?conditions.? Starting in?R2014a, the code generation output is correct for these conditions, but the simulation output is wrong.
Workaround: If the table is input from a Constant block,?select the Interpret vector parameter as 1-D check box for the?Constant block.Otherwise:Insert a Reshape block, with the Output dimensionality parameter set to?1-D array,?between the Table input port (T)?of the Direct Lookup Table (n-D) block and the?block that feeds the table port T.For an example of this workaround, see the attached model.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Code and Simulation mismatch when Simulink Function is in an algebraic loop
Description: The behavior of the code generated for a model may not match the behavior of a simulation of the model?when all of following conditions are true:? The model contains a Simulink Function block An output port of the Simulink Function block is connected directly to an input port of that Simulink Function block Inside the Simulink Function, the Outport block corresponding to that Simulink Function?block output port is connected directly to an ArgIn  port
Workaround: Place a Signal Conversion block after the ArgIn port and select the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization checkbox.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 3, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable reuse when a Unit Delay block connects to a Constant block inside an enabled, triggered, or function-call subsystem
Description: When a Unit Delay block is inside a triggered, function-call, or enabled subsystem, the state variable of the Unit Delay block?may be incorrectly reused?if all of the following conditions are true: O ptimize global data access ?configuration parameter is set to?Use global to hold temporary results ?or?Minimize global data access . R euse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled. The Unit Delay input signal also connects to a subsystem outport block, which has null initial conditions. The Unit Delay input signal is driven by a Constant or Initial Condition block. The initial conditions of the Unit Delay and driving block do not match.
Workaround: See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727. That report describes a different but related issue, and the same workaround applies.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Reusable?custom storage class?specification at the input of a Unit Delay block can lead to incorrect code
Description: In code, the state of a Unit Delay block may be incorrectly reused with a reusable custom storage class variable if all of the following are true: Optimize global data access ?configuration parameter is set to Use global to hold temporary results or Minimize global data access   . Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled. The input to?a Unit Delay block has a reusable?custom storage class?specification. That input signal is also connected to a root output port. The?same reusable?custom storage class?specification is at the root input port.  For the attached example, the incorrect code generation pattern is as follows: ?abc = abc + abc;  Here, abc  is a variable corresponding to reusable custom storage class signal specification. In this example, the state variable got incorrectly merged into abc .
Workaround: To workaround around the issue, do either of the following:See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727. That report describes a different but related issue, and the same workaround applies.Remove the?reusable custom storage class?specification from the input of the Unit Delay block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink Function blocks?that specify shared input/output argument might reuse wrong input in generated code
Description: If a Simulink Function block with input/output reuse has any another input with the same datatype, complexity, and size as the reused input, the generated code might call the?function?with incorrect arguments. Specifically, the generated code might reuse the wrong variable at the Function Caller block?and produce a wrong answer. This occurs even if the other inputs are also specified to reuse with another output. In the below example, the?Simulink Function?f  has a 2 inputs u ?and x , and two outputs y ?and x .? The input/output?have the same?name, so?a single argument?is used in the function for the input/output pair. The generated code for the function definition looks similar to the following code: /* Output and update for Simulink Function: '<Root>/Simulink Function' */  void f(real_T rtu_u, real_T *rty_y, real_T *rtuy_x)  {  ? ...  ?The problem occurs at the function call site.? Here is an example of incorrect code: ? ...  ? /* FunctionCaller: '<Root>/Function Caller' */  ? rtb_inoutarg = rtb_input2;  ? f(rtb_input2, &rtb_output1, &rtb_inoutarg);  ? ... ? The first statement occurs because Simulink cannot reuse the same variable on the shared input/output -- a copy of the input is introduced.? The problem is that the two copies of input x  are passed to the Simulink function as inputs x  and u . ?The correct code uses a different variable for input u  and appears as follows: ? ...  ? /* FunctionCaller: '<Root>/Function Caller'? */  ??rtb_inoutarg?=?rtb_input2;  ? f(rtb_input1, &rtb_output1, &rtb_inoutarg);  ? ...
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 2, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect results might occur when the sample times of the input and output of a Unit Delay block are different and?Optimize global data access?parameter is set to a value other than?none
Description: Incorrect code might be generated for a Unit Delay block if all of the following conditions are true: The sample time of the Unit Delay's input is different from the sample time of the destination block of the Unit Delay's output. Optimize global data access  configuration parameter is set to Use global to hold temporary results  or Minimize global data access.  Reuse local block outputs  configuration parameter is enabled.  When these conditions are met, the generated code might?contain the wrong value for the?Unit Delay block's?output.??
Workaround: See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727. That report describes a different but related issue, and the same workaround applies.?

Summary: Unused local variables in model initialization function
Description: Simulink Coder can generate unused local variables in the model_initialize  function under these following conditions: The model contains a user written S-function that generates C/C++ code utilizing a TLC implementation. The user written S-function implements TLC based expression folding. The user written S-function TLC implementation has a block initialization function.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2018a Update 4, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable reuse when a Model block or Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block
Description: When a Model block or Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block, the state variable may be incorrectly reused if the following conditions are true: In the case of the Model block, inside the Model block, the root outport block that drives the Unit Delay block has non-zero initial value (for example, it is driven by a Constant block) that is different from the initial value of the state variable.  In the case of the Probe block, the Probe block connects to a Unit Delay block, and the initial value is not equal to the signal size that the Probe block acts on.  Optimize global data access  configuration parameter is set to Use global to hold temporary results or Minimize global data access ; or Reuse global block outputs  configuration parameter is enabled.  Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled.  When these conditions are true, the model block output or Probe block output and Unit Delay state may get merged, resulting in an incorrect answer because the mismatched initial conditions were merged.?
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generated for the n-D Lookup table block with Interpolation method?Nearest?and Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code?check box selected
Description: Incorrect?code for the n-D Lookup table block can generate incorrect output when all of the following conditions are true:? The Interpolation method ?is set to Nearest  The Index search method ?is set to Linear   search  The Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code ?check box is selected The value of any input is nearest?to the last index value ofits breakpoints
Workaround: Do one of the following:Set the?Index search method?to be?Binary?searchClear?the?Remove protection against out-of-range input in generated code?check boxReplace the n-D Lookup Table block with one or more Prelookup blocks connected to?an Interpolation Using Prelookup block?and match the corresponding settings
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Incorrect code may be generated for fraction output of Prelookup block?in models containing multiple similar Prelookup blocks
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for the?fraction output of Prelookup blocks?under these conditions Model contains at least two Prelookup blocks Index search method  of each Prelookup block is set to Evenly spaced points  Data type of input signal and Breakpoints  data type?are not the same Breakpoints  data type of each block is a fixed-point data type The word length of?one or more?fixed-point data type differs from the word length of the other Breakpoints  data types The word length of each of the fixed-point data types must have the same container word length
Workaround: Set the word length of?all Prelookup Breakpoints data types to the same?length.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2018a Update 5, R2018b

Summary: Test cases might fail to achieve intended model coverage for objectives impacted by 'mod' operator
Description: If your model consists of 'mod' operator computations, test generation analysis incorrectly analyzes the objectives impacted by the 'mod' operator. Simulink Design Verifier reports the status of such objectives as 'Undecided with testcases' , and the test cases that the software generates might fail to achieve the intended model coverage.

Summary: Proof Objective, Proof Assumption, Test objective and Test condition blocks produce incorrect answers for enumeration values derived from built-in integer types
Description: The Values  field of a Proof Objective, Proof Assumption, Test Objective or Test Condition block accepts enumerations. However, when a Proof Objective, Proof Assumption, Test Objective, or Test Condition block uses an enumeration not derived from Simulink.IntEnumType, such as int8 and uint16, the results are incorrect.  In cases where the enumeration is not derived from Simulink.IntEnumType:  The Proof Objective is always proven valid. The Test Objective cannot be satisfied The Proof Assumption and Test Condition have no effect.
Workaround: If you are using R2015aSP1 or later, and want to keep the enumeration as a subclass of a built-in integer type, such as int8 and uint16, insert a Compare To Constant block with the Operator ==, and set the Constant value to the enumeration value. Connect the Compare to constant block to the Proof Objective, Proof Assumption, Test objective or Test condition block, and set its value to True.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Simulink model with Variant subsystem?might incorrectly generate preprocessor conditionals
Description: Simulink model with Variant subsystem might generate code without preprocessor conditionals when all?of the following conditions are true:  Variant Subsystem is within a conditionally executed subsystem.  Variant Subsystem has an empty default choice.  Option?Analyze all choices during update diagram and generate preprocessor conditionals ?is selected.
Workaround: Place System Outputs block from the Simulink Library Browser inside the empty choice to workaround the behavior.?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for multitasking mode
Description: Incorrect generated code is possible for a?model that meets these conditions: In Solver  pane, under Additional parameters , the?Treat each discrete rate as a separate task  parameter is on. In the same pane, the?Automatically handle rate transition for data transfer  is off. In the?Diagnostics >Sample Time  pane, the?Multitask rate transition  parameter is set to warning . In the model, at least one root outport block has a different discrete sample time?than the block output port driving it.
Workaround: In the?Solver?pane,?under?Additional parameters,?select the?Automatically handle rate transition for data transfer parameter.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks incorrectly share same prelookup function?for some fixed-point inputs and breakpoints
Description: The Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks might incorrectly share the same prelookup?function when the following condition is?true for two or more Lookup Table?(n-D) or Prelookup blocks in a model: Inputs and breakpoints both specify fixed-point data types with the?same?container word length , positive fixed exponent, and slope adjustment factor, but with different word length.  The incorrectly shared prelookup function might lead to wrong results?from the generated code.

Summary: Incorrect code may be generated for a For Iterator Subsystem containing an Assignment block with the input port <Y0> enabled
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for a model that meets these conditions: The model contains a For Iterator subsystem. The For Iterator subsystem contains an Assignment block with the input port <Y0>  enabled. The immediate data source feeding into the input port <Y0>  of the Assignment block is located outside the For Iterator subsystem. The aforementioned immediate data source is either a Mux block or a Bus Creator block configured to output a virtual bus signal.  In the incorrect generated C/C++ code, ?the variable corresponding to the output of the Assignment block is initialized by an uninitialized local variable.
Workaround: As a workaround, insert a Signal Conversion block with the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization parameter selected in front of the input port <Y0> of the Assignment block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable reuse when a Unit Delay input or output is connected to a subystem's output signal and the?Optimize global data access parameter is set to a value other than none
Description: In the code, the state variable for a Unit Delay block might be incorrectly reused, leading to wrong answers, if all of the following conditions are true:? Optimize global data access ?configuration parameter is set to?Use global to hold temporary results ?or?Minimize global data access . Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled. A model contains a subsystem in which the?Function packaging ?parameter is set to?Auto ?or?Reusable, or is set to  NonReusable?and? Function interface  ?is set?to  Allow Arguments.   A Unit Delay block's input or output is connected to that subsystem's output signal.    In releases R2015b to R2017b, Delay blocks configured with Length  = 1 are also affected by this bug. Errors might be thrown during the build process.? See the attached?BugReport1668727Details.zip for more information, including models that demonstrate the issue.
Workaround: To work around this issue, as well as the those described in Bug Report IDs?1284809, 1286055, 1287804, 1301976, 1373141, 1649889, 1658667, 1771523, 1773229, and 1773232, do one of the following:?Unzip the contents of attachment_1668727_15a_thru_17b_2018-04-10.zip to a folder on the MATLAB path. Each time you start MATLAB, enter the following in the MATLAB Command Window:>>? workaroundForBugReport1668727Set the?Optimize global data access?configuration parameter to?None. Note that changing this configuration parameter setting disables more optimizations than the above workaround and can result in less efficient code.?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible?when an Assignment block?connects to a Unit Delay block
Description: Incorrect generated code is possible for a model that meets these conditions An Assignment block that has a Y0  port?connects to the output of a Unit Delay block (or a Delay Block with a Delay Length  value of 1 ) The output of the Assignment block connects directly to the input of the same Unit Delay block. Both blocks are inside a While Iterator, For Iterator, or For Each subsystem. The?Optimize Global Data Access? parameter is set to Use global to hold temporary results  or Minimize global data access.   When?a model?meets the above conditions, the state for the Unit Delay block may be a local variable in the generated code. This causes the state to be uninitialized every time the function gets called causing incorrect answers. For the attached example, the code generator?generates the following incorrect code:? void Model_1658667_step(void){? int32_T s1_iter;?  real_T UnitDelay_DSTATE[4];  ?? for (s1_iter = 0; s1_iter + 1 <= rtP.ForIterator_IterationLimit; s1_iter++) {?? ? UnitDelay_DSTATE[s1_iter] += rtU.In1;?? ? rtY.Out1[0] = rtP.Gain_Gain * UnitDelay_DSTATE[0];?? ? rtY.Out1[1] = rtP.Gain_Gain * UnitDelay_DSTATE[1];?? ? rtY.Out1[2] = rtP.Gain_Gain * UnitDelay_DSTATE[2];?? ? rtY.Out1[3] = rtP.Gain_Gain * UnitDelay_DSTATE[3];??}}?
Workaround: See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727. That report describes a different but related issue, and the same workaround applies.

Summary: State variable for Unit Delay incorrectly overwritten for a subsystem whose function interface includes arguments
Description: See?Bug Report ID 1668727 ?for full details on related issues.? Incorrect code generation might occur when all of the following conditions are true: Optimize global data access ?configuration parameter is set to?Use global to hold temporary results ?or?Minimize global data access  Reuse local block outputs ?configuration parameter is enabled. The model contains a subsystem in which the?Function packaging ?parameter set to?NonReusable?and Function interface ?is set to  Allow Arguments.   A Unit Delay block's input or output is connected to that subsystem's output signal.  Errors might be thrown during the build process.?
Workaround: See the workaround in?Bug Report ID 1668727.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 7, R2017b Update 4, R2018a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible?for temporary variables with?constant value indexing inside?of a?for loop body
Description: The generated code may be incorrect for a model that results in generated code which meets all of these conditions: The generated code contains a for  loop. Inside of the for  loop, there is a temporary variable with constant index used to carry scalar value across loop iteration After the for  loop, there are no further references to the temporary variable.  In the incorrect generated code, the temporary variable contains an incorrect value because it is not updated with each iteration of the for  loop. For?example this is the?incorrect code?pattern:? for (i=0; i<8; i++) {?? a=A[5];??? if (2.0 +?i <= y[12+i]) {????????? x=a;??? }??? a=x+1;?}?  This is the correct code pattern:? ?for (i=0; i<8; i++) {?? a=A[5];??? if (2.0 +?i <= y[12+i]) {????????? x=a;??? }??? a=x+1;????A[5]=a; }?  In the correct code pattern, the value of the temporary variable A  is updated at the end of each iteration of the for  loop. ?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for AUTOSAR target when an S-function sets?ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW?to?TRUE
Description: Incorrect code may be generated for a model when all these conditions are met:? The system target file is set to AUTOSAR.tlc, AND The system has an S-function with the ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW(SimStruct *S, int idx, bool val)  function having the bool  argument val set to 1 (TRUE)? for at least one Outport block, AND The S-function must be connected to a root input port.  In the generated code, the input variable for the S-Function may be incorrectly reused.
Workaround: There are three workarounds, each of which will have impact on code efficiency (increasing stack size):Disable the configuration parameter Reuse local block outputs.?Set the bool?argument of ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW to 0 (FALSE) for all the identified S-function blocks.??Insert a Signal Conversion block at the input of all affected S-Function blocks that set the bool argument of ssSetOutputPortOutputExprInRTW to 1 (TRUE). Enable the parameter?Excludethis block from ˇ®Block reductionˇŻ optimization on the Block Parameters?dialog box?for each insertedSignal Conversion block. Regenerate and inspect the code to confirm that theproblem is averted.?
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016b Update 6, R2017a Update 3, R2017b Update 6, R2018a

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Reusable functions generated from subsystems connected to root inports with different bus types can?get reused?incorrectly in generated?code
Description: If you?configure multiple atomic subsystems?to use the same reusable function for code generation (Function packaging  set to?Auto , Reuseable?function ) and the subsystems are connected to root inports that have different bus types that have some overlapping elements, the code generator?might produce one function definition and a call to that function for each subsystem. The code that results can produce silent wrong answers.  The code generator should produce a separate function definition for each subsystem for which the inport data type varies.
Workaround: For?atomic subsystems that are connected to inports that are buses with different data types, set the subsystem block parameter Function packaging to Inline.?Although the code size increases, the code generator inlines the correct function body for each subsystem.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code for schur,sqrtm, and eig may produce wrong answers

When the issue occurs in a generated MEX function, the MEX function issues the warning " SCHUR failed. " ?
Workaround: Installation instructions  Log in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.  Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps. On Mac systems, the folder name will have a .app extension, e.g. /Applications/MATLAB_R20XXa.app  Exit MATLAB.  Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Windows Systems: You may need administrative privileges to save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder. To do this, open this Bug Report in a browser started in administrator mode, then from the browser save the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Mac Systems: If you are using Safari on a Mac, disable automatic unzipping of the downloaded file by unchecking Open Safe Files After Downloading in Preferences > General.   For each file that appears in the .zip file, find the corresponding file in your existing <MATLAB> folder and make a backup of it. To see the contents of the .zip file:          Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.      Linux/Mac Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Mac):        unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>On a Mac, include the .app extension in the folder name.         Extract the files from the .zip file into the <MATLAB> folder.          Windows Systems:                  Open the .zip file.          Extract the contents of the file into your <MATLAB> folder. Products: MATLAB Coder, Simulink Coder, Embedded Coder

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code may be generated when Discrete-Time Integrator's State Port is used and Single output/update function parameter is selected
Description: Incorrect code may occur for a model that meets these conditions: The model contains a Discrete-Time Integrator block. On the?Discrete-Time Integrator block parameters dialog box, the State Port  parameter is?selected. The State Port output connects to a Goto block. The From block that corresponds with the Goto block is in another atomic subsystem. In the Configuration parameters dialog box, the Single output/update function  parameter is selected. The?Discrete-Time Integrator block and state port destination execute in the same time step.  The demo model sldemo_clutch  contains activated state ports and corresponding Goto and From blocks that cross atomic subsystem boundaries. This model demonstrates?how to correctly?use state ports that cross atomic subsystem boundaries -- the locked and unlocked subsystems are never enabled in the same time step. While the model contains the continuous time version of the Integrator block, you could use the Discrete-Time version, and the model would still generate correct code because of the exclusive execution contexts. However if a model?executes both the Discrete-Time Integrator?block and its state port destination in the same time step, the code is incorrect? because the generated code uses the newly calculated state, rather than the state from the previous time step as simulation would do.
Workaround: Clear the?Single output/update function parameter for the model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mismatch in answer with MATLAB?when the input to isreal?is an object, a structure, or a function handle
Description: If?x  is an object, a structure, or a function handle,? isreal(x)  returns true  in the generated code, but false  in MATLAB. This issue affects MATLAB Coder, MATLAB Function blocks or MATLAB System blocks in Simulink, ?and MATLAB functions in Stateflow.
Workaround: Modify the source code to avoid dependency of the return value of isreal when the input is an?object, a structure, or a ?function handle.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Casting tological from a fixed-point?type with slope bias scaling?that cannot represent zero canproduce the wrong answer
Description: In MATLAB, you candefine a fixed-point?type with slope bias scaling?that cannot represent zero and cast thistype to a logical type. In this operation, the correct logical result is true ,since the source value can never be zero. However, code generation produces false .This issue can occur in MATLAB Coder, or for a MATLAB Function block or a MATLAB System block in Simulink.  Here is an example that illustrates the issue:   x ?=cast(fi(1,?1,?32,?2,?-1),?'logical');?  This cast operationproduces true  in MATLAB, but generated code can compute false .?
Workaround: Avoidcasting to logical type for fixed-point types with slope bias scaling?that cannot representzero.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for an inlined For Each subsystem with separate output and update functions
Description: Incorrect generated code may occur for a model that meets these conditions: The model contains a For Each subsystem block. In the Configuration Parameters dialog box, the Single output/update function  parameter is deselected, the Language  is set to C  (not C++ ), and Function Prototype Control is not?configured for the model. The For Each subsystem is?inside of another subsystem that is a Function-Call, Enabled, or Triggered subsystem. The For Each subsystem has an internal state because it uses an?Integrator block or a Delay block, for example. The For Each subsystem Function packaging  is set to Inline  or Auto  with a single instance so that the code generator inlines the For Each subsystem code.  When a model meets these conditions, the parent subsystem function code contains two for  loops. In the incorrect generated code,?a?scalar variable (in the below example, INPUT_TO_DELAY )?is used to?calculate the next state in the first loop and store it for the second loop. Using a scalar variable causes the state calculation to use the last array element for the entire array as shown here: void Subsystem() {?  ??? real_T INPUT_TO_DELAY;  ??? for (ImplicitIterator_itr = 0; ImplicitIterator_itr < 5;???????? ImplicitIterator_itr++) {  ????? INPUT_TO_DELAY = ...  ??? }  ??? for (ImplicitIterator_itr = 0; ImplicitIterator_itr < 5;???????? ImplicitIterator_itr++) {????? debug1_DW.CoreSubsys[ImplicitIterator_itr].UnitDelay_DSTATE = INPUT_TO_DELAY;??? }?}  In the correct generated code, an array is used to store each state element. That way, each state element gets?the correct calculated value.  void Subsystem() {?  ??? real_T INPUT_TO_DELAY[5];  ??? for (ImplicitIterator_itr = 0; ImplicitIterator_itr < 5;???????? ImplicitIterator_itr++) {  ????? INPUT_TO_DELAY[ImplicitIterator_itr] = ...  ??? }  ??? for (ImplicitIterator_itr = 0; ImplicitIterator_itr < 5;???????? ImplicitIterator_itr++) {????? debug1_DW.CoreSubsys[ImplicitIterator_itr].UnitDelay_DSTATE = INPUT_TO_DELAY[ImplicitIterator_itr];??? }?}
Workaround: To avoid this bug, select the Single output/update function parameter.? If your model requires separate output/update functions, for the For Each subsystem block, change the Function packaging parameter?to Reusable function or Nonreusable function.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Breakpoint data type not propagated to input signal for n-D Lookup Table block
Description: Breakpoint data type not propagated to input signal for n-D Lookup Table block. In some cases, no error is returned. Default propagation of input signals for the n-D Lookup Table block does not produce the same data type as the corresponding breakpoint in the case of breakpoint data with fixed-point data types. In these cases: No error is returned if the slope adjustment factors of the table and breakpoint are the same and the?breakpoint data type does not?correctly backpropagate to the?input signal for the block.?? This error is returned during model update?if the?slope adjustment factor of the table data does not match?the slope adjustment factor of any?breakpoints:  The input and breakpoint data types of block '<n-D Lookup Table block>' do not have the same SlopeAdjustmentFactor. The breakpoint data type has a SlopeAdjustmentFactor of?N  and the input data type has a SlopeAdjustmentFactor of M .
Workaround: As a workaround,??if n-D Lookup Table does not produce the same data type as the input source, Add a Data Type Conversion block between the source of the input and the input port of the n-D Lookup Table block.For the Data Type Conversion block,?set Output data type to the same data type as the corresponding breakpoint of the connected n-D Lookup Table block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code may be incorrect for a Constant block with a sample time other than inf that branches to multiple destinations
Description: The generated code may be incorrect for a Constant block that meets these conditions: The Constant block has a sample time other than inf .? The signal line that leaves the Constant block branches?to multiple destinations.? The Constant block?or one of its destinations has an asynchronous rate, or the?Constant block has a discrete rate, and one of its destinations has a faster discrete rate.?
Workaround: You can choose?either of these?two workarounds:In the Constant block parameters dialog box, set the Sample time parameter to Inf.??Place a Signal Conversion block at the output of the Constant block. In the Signal Conversion block parameters dialog box,?select Exclude this block from Block reduction optimization'.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect logic in generated code when atomic subsystem has a single outport fed by a block conditionally writing to its output
Description: If you generate code for a model with an atomic subsystem in which all of the following conditions are true, the generated code might contain incorrect logic involving the subsystem output.In the Subsystem Block Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation  tab, the Function packaging  parameter is set to Nonreusable function . The Function interface  parameter is set to Allows arguments .  The atomic subsystem has only one outport, and the signal leaving this outport is a scalar. The atomic subsystem outport is either fed by control flow logic in which not all possible execution paths are defined (for example, but not limited to, an If block without an else port, or a Switch Case block without a default port), or by a block that does not write to its output every time step.  Under these conditions a function might be generated for the atomic subsystem in which the output is passed by value, and that output is not initialized nor written to on all execution paths through the function.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: The Pulse Generator block could output incorrect pulses for simulation and generated code
Description: The Pulse Generator block could output incorrect pulses for simulation and generated code when the following conditions are met, 1. ?The Pulse Generator block has 'Pulse type' set to be 'Time based'. 2. The value of the parameter 'Period' is not exact multiples of the solver's step. For example, it is set to be '5e-5' while the solver step is '1e-6', or the calculated 'Pulse Width' is not multiples of the solver step. Given the above, the output pulse could have wrong period or pulse width.

Summary: MATLAB throws an error when trying to generate code for a model with reusable subsystem connected to a block using TLC
Description: MATLAB throws an error when you try to generate code for a model that meets all of the following conditions: The model contains at least one reusable subsystem The reusable subsystem has one of its outputs branched and connected to a block that uses TLC for code generation The block has reference to its input signal in its TLC implementation

CheckBias   property for a fixed-point conceptual argument is?set to?false .  You generate code for a Simulink model or MATLAB function that contains fixed-point?types with?non-zero bias.

Summary: Models with Rate Limiter block and model reference can crash while doing simulation
Description: For Simulink models having model reference, a Rate Limiter block in the referenced model, and different solver types in the top and referenced models, crashes during simulation.
Workaround: The workaround is to use the same non auto solver for the top and the referenced models. If the referenced model does not have any continuous states, it will switch to a discrete solver, so use an explicit solver (ode45 for variable step and ode3 for fixed step) for the top model. This can be seen by doing update diagram (Ctrl-D) on the referenced model, and the solver displayed at the bottom right corner will change to a discrete solver (VariableStepDiscrete or FixedStepDiscrete).

Summary: Incorrect Relational Boundary coverage for saturation block with differing fixed-point input/output datatypes
Description: When the input and output signals of a Saturation block are configured to use different fixed-point datatypes, the Relational Boundary coverage results reported for this block might be incorrect due to an improper datatype conversion by the Model Coverage tool. This bug may cause satisfied Relational Boundary coverage objectives to be reported as unsatisifed and vice-versa. For example, consider a Saturation block with an Upper limit  set to 0.5  and Output data type  set to fixdt(1, 16, 7)  which represents a signed fixed-point datatype with word length of 16 and fraction length of 7. ?The input to the Saturation block comes from a Constant block with a value of 0.5  and Output data type  set to ?fixdt(1, 16, 8) ? representing a signed fixed-point datatype with word length of 16 and fraction length of 8. ?Note the difference in the fraction length used by the input and output datatypes. When simulated, we would expect the Coverage analysis to report that the Saturation block's Relational Boundary objective 'input - upper limit ' would be satisfied for outcome 0  (indicating that the input signal and upper limit are equal). ?However,?the Coverage analysis reports that none of the Relational Boundary objective outcomes for this block are satisfied.
Workaround: When the input signal to a Saturation block uses a fixed-point datatype, ensure that the block's output makes use of the same datatype. ?This can be done by setting the Saturation block's field Output data type to 'Inherit: Same as input'.If it is necessary for the output datatype to differ, consider instead connecting the output of the Saturation block to a Data Type Conversion block rather than explicitly setting the datatype via the Output data type field of the Saturation block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for a subsystem with a single output and nonreusable function packaging with arguments
Description: For a model that contains a subsystem that meets the following conditions, the code generator might incorrectly pass the subsystem output through the function return value instead of by reference: 1. The subsystem?has a single output. 2. In the Subsystem Block Parameters dialog box, on the Code Generation  tab, the Function packaging  parameter is set to Nonreusable function . The Function interface  parameter is set to Allows arguments . 3. Inside the subsystem, the subsystem output is conditionally written. 4. The subsystem output is a scalar.

Description: The following incorrect Mfx_Div*  replacements can occur when generating code using the AUTOSAR 4.0 CRL library:       In1 Type      In2 Type      Output Type      Incorrect Mfx_Div* replacement      Affected release           uint8      int32      uint16      Mfx_Div_s32u16_u16      R2014b - R2016b           int16      int32      int16      Mfx_Div_s32s16_s16      R2014b - R2016b           uint16      int32      uint16      Mfx_Div_s32u16_u16      R2014b - R2016b           uint8      uint32      uint16      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u16      R2014b           uint8      uint32      uint32      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u32      R2014b           uint16      uint32      uint16      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u16      R2014b           uint16      uint32      uint32      Mfx_Div_u32u16_u32      R2014b
Workaround: Visit Bug Report ID 1435708 to download a patch that addresses this and other bugs.

Summary: Possible wrong answer?for?simulation and code generation?for Sum block whose Accumulator data type value is fixed-point with?bias
Description: Possible wrong answer for simulation and code generation might occur?under these conditions: Model contains a Sum block The Sum block has Accumulator data type  parameter set to a fixed-point data type?with?bias The Sum block has Output data type ?parameter set to single  or double .
Workaround: Set the??Output data type?parameter of the Sum block to?Inherit: Same as accumulator.Add and connect a?Data Type Conversion block to the output of the Sum block.Set the Output data type parameter of the?Data Type Conversion block to single or double.

Summary: MATLAB crashes when simulating? the?Simulink Math Function block with Function set to?mod or rem
Description: MATLAB crashes when simulating?the?Simulink Math Function block with Function  set to?mod  or rem and if the block has these   X ,Y  input combinations:?  ?X ?is?intmin('int32')  or int32(-2147483648)  ?Y ?is int32(-1)
Workaround: Use one of these workarounds:?Convert ?the input data type to a floating-point single or double before mod or rem operations.Write equivalent code?using a MATLAB Function block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Global storage class with alias on reusable subsystem output signal may?cause incorrect code generation
Description: For a model that meets the following conditions, the code generator might generate incorrect code for the reusable subsystem output function: The model contains at least two instances of the same reusable subsystem. Both reusable subsystem instances has an output signal annotated with the same global storage class The global storage class has a different alias for each reusable subsystem instance.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: AUTOSAR 4.0 code replacement library Mfx_MulShRight_* entries can provide incorrect calculation
Description: AUTOSAR 4.0 code replacement library Mfx_MulShRight_*  entries can provide incorrect calculation for a fixed-point multiplication operation The Mfx_MulShRight_*  entries calculate the net slope adjustment factor incorrectly  as: ?????output_slope_adjustment_factor * input1_slope_adjustment_factor / input2_slope_adjustment_factor   The correct net slope adjustment factor calculation is:  ?????input1_slope_adjustment_factor * input2_slope_adjustment_factor / output_slope_adjustment_factor  When the correct/ true  net slope adjustment factor is NOT equal to 1 but the incorrect  net slope adjustment factor is equal to 1 so that a Mfx_MulShRight_*  replacement occurs, the code replacement in the generated code can produce incorrect numerical results.
Workaround: Visit Bug Report ID 1435708 to download a patch that addresses this and other bugs.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code may occur when virtual blocks reorder the input data to reduced block
Description: Incorrect generated code may occur for a model that meets the following conditions:?   The optimization parameters Block Reduction  and Signal Storage?Reuse  are set to on .? Block reduction  removes a block in an active code path. Blocks?that are on?dead code paths are not susceptible to this bug. The input data to the reduced block is not in the same order as the data source. This could happen for a model that contains a Mux block or a Bus Creator block that outputs a virtual bus. The input to the Mux block or Bus Creator block are elements of a single buffer. These elements could be the?result of a Demux block or a Bus Selector block. The blocks participating in the above code path are part of the same atomic subsystem.  In the incorrect generated code, the buffer of the block driving the reduced block?may be?incorrectly reused?such that?another block overwrites the value before all uses of the original block execute. The?workaround section contains an attached example?model that?demonstrates this bug. ?In the example model, the demux/mux structure reorders elements of the Sum block before they are input to the Gain block.??In the generated code, the Gain block is reduced because it has a value of 1 . ??The Subtract block?requires the results of the Sum and Product?blocks. The Product block reuses the?Sum block?buffer. As a result, the Subtract block incorrectly uses the second element of the result of the?Product block instead of the second element of the result of the Sum block. The code snippet below shows this incorrect code (generated using R2014a without the workaround): ? ?for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] = g1475400_U.In1[i] + g1475400_ConstP.Constant_Value[i]; // Sum Block output   ? ? g1475400_Y.Out3[i] = g1475400_Y.Out1[i] + -4.0;  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] *= g1475400_U.In2[i]; // Overwritten by Product Block output   ? ? g1475400_Y.Out2[i] = g1475400_Y.Out1[i] + -3.0;  ? }    ? tmp = g1475400_Y.Out1[1]; // Ends up getting result of Product Block rather than Sum Block  ? for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) {  ? ? g1475400_Y.Out1[i] = (tmp - g1475400_Y.Out1[i]) + -2.0;  ? }      Examples of blocks that can be removed by block reduction - these will all be cases where the actual data is unchanged by the block calculation:  Gain blocks with a non-tunable value of 1   Concatenate blocks with a single input Absolute value blocks with an unsigned  input datatype Reshape blocks Shift block with shift amount of 0  Permute block with dimensions specified for no permutation Single input Logical AND / OR block Bias block with bias amount of 0  Switch block with non-tunable constant threshold and control port is constant? Delay block with delay 0
Workaround: To avoid this bug, you can use the attached patch to disable some optimizations?pertaining to?virtual bus selection. ?To do so, insert the p-file in the MATLAB path and each time you start MATLAB, enter the following in the MATLAB Command Window:run?workaround_g1475400.pDisabling these?optimizations?may result in more memory usage and data copies for virtual buses, but the code efficiency for demux/mux operations?does not change.Alternatively, you can?set the Block Reduction parameter to off. Changing this parameter?setting will likely result in much more memory usage and data copies than the using the attached patch.
Fixed In: R2015aSP1 Update 3, R2016a Update 7, R2016b Update 6, R2017a

Summary: Subsystem output is incorrectly initialized in certain cases when subsystem connects to a Merge block
Description: The output of a conditionally executed subsystem will be incorrectly initialized when all the following conditions are true: The Outport block of the subsystem has an initial value. The subsystem output corresponding to the above outport connects to multiple blocks outside the subsystem, either through a Bus Selector or a Demux block. At least one of the blocks connected to the output of the subsystem is not a Merge block.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible loss of precision when constant folding large?int64/uint64?inputs to binary max and min functions in code generation
Description: During code generation from MATLAB code, constant folding binary calls to the functions?max ?and?min  with int64  or uint64  inputs might result in a loss of precision. The loss of precision is?due to wrong answers from?intermediate conversions to double.?Note that the issue occurs only when the call to max  and min  is constant folded. For example, the code:   function [y,z] = foo()  x = int64(9007199254741017);  y = coder.const(max(x,x));  z = max(x,x); returns y  as 9007199254741016 ?and  z  as?9007199254741017, because y  is computed using constant folding and z  is not. This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are true: int64 ?or?uint64 ?values are passed to?max ?or?min . One or more of these 64-bit integers is not representable in double. Only values larger than flintmax ?can?trigger this issue.  max ?or?min ?is comparing elements of?two arrays such as max(1:3,3:5) . The syntax that computes the minimum or maximum of a single array does not trigger this issue.  Note that this can occur for calls to?max ?or?min ?that appear directly in your code or in code used by your code.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Switch statements using an enumeration with nonunique members may produce a wrong answer in generated code
Description: The generated code may produce a wrong answer when a switch?statement?uses an enumeration where multiple members have the same value. In this case, the code generator might compare an enumeration switch expression by enumeration member order?rather than by explicit enumeration member value.?? Consider the following example:? classdef Letters < int8    enumeration        A(1)        B(0)        C(0)    endendfunction result = main(letter)result = coder.const(@foo, letter);function result = foo(arg)switch arg    case Letters.A        result = 0;    case Letters.C        result = 2;    case Letters.B        result = 1;    otherwise        result = 3;end Calling main(Letters.B)  in?MATLAB displays 2. However, calling the MEX function generated by MATLAB Coder, main_mex(Letters.B), displays 3 .  When this problem occurs, the code generator issues a warning: Warning: Redundant SWITCH cases. Only the first will be executed. If this warning is issued when using an enumeration with nonunique enumeration member values, a wrong answer is possible. This issue can occur in MATLAB Coder, a MATLAB Function Block, MATLAB in Stateflow, and a MATLAB System Block.
Workaround: If you do not see the warning for "Redundant SWITCH cases" then you are not affected by this issue. If you see the warning, make sure that each enumeration member of an enumeration?has a?unique value.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect generated code is possible for atomic subsystem with no outports and with Minimize algebraic loop occurrences parameter set to on
Description: The sorted order may be incorrect for a model containing an atomic subsystem with no outports and with the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences  parameter set to on . For example, the?atomic subsystem may occur before the blocks that drive it even though the atomic subsystem is a direct-feed-through block. The incorrect sorted order may cause the following: 1. If the Enable local block outputs  parameter is set to on , the simulation and code generation results may not match because the generated code may contain a local variable whose value is used but not assigned.  2.??If the Enable local block outputs  parameter is set to off , there may be a hidden wrong answer because both the simulation and code generation results are incorrect.
Workaround: There are two possible workarounds:??(1)??Use the function turnOffMinAlgLoopFlagForSubsysWithoutOutports in the attached zip file?to automatically set MinAlgLoopOccurrences flag to??off?for subsystems without outports.?(2) ?Manually set the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences parameter to off?by entering the following command:? ? ? ? set_param(block, 'MinAlgLoopOccurrences', 'off');??Please note this parameter's default setting?is off, and this parameter is not shown?on the subsystem block parameters dialog box. ?
Products: MATLAB Coder, Embedded Coder, Simulink Coder

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code for S-function with non-scalar signal input can produce incorrect numerical results
Description: The generated code for the S-function block (built with system target file rtwsfcn.tlc ) can produce incorrect numerical results when the following conditions are true: The generated S-function block?has at least 1 input port.  One or more input ports to the S-function block has a non-scalar dimension.  Multiple upstream blocks contribute to the non-scalar signal line that connects to the input port or ports of the S-function block. For example, a Mux block creates the non-scalar signal line.   These conditions can cause the input signal data to the S-function block to reside in non-contiguous memory. Because the S-function requires that the input data is contiguous, the generated code can produce incorrect numerical results.
Workaround: Use either of these workaroundsIf using a previously generated S-function block, insert a Signal Conversion block immediately before each non-scalar input to the S-function. This Signal Conversion block directs the code generator to make a copy of the signal in a contiguous memory region that the S-function reads. Configure the Signal Conversion block as follows:Set Output: to Signal CopyEnable Exclude this block from 'Block Reduction' optimizationIf generating the S-function block (with the S-function system target file rtwsfcn.tlc), before generating code, configure either of these Configuration Parameters > Optimization > Signals and Parameters options for the block as follows. Either of these options cause the generated code for the S-function not to?expect a contiguous memory region for non-scalar inputs:Disable Use memcpy for vector assignmentSet the Memcpy threshold value to a large number (for example, intmax: 2147483647)

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable assignment to 0.0 or -0.0 in the true and false branches of if statement
Description: The code generator may generate incorrect C code for a Simulink model that contains the following MATLAB code:  if p?  ?? y=0;?  else?  ?? y=-0;?  end  The C code may be optimized such  that y  is set equal to 0.0  or -0.0  regardless of whether the If  statement evaluates to true or false. Note that the above MATLAB code may be explicit in a Simulink model (in a MATLAB function block, for example). The above MATLAB code may also occur during the code generation process and thus be in the model as a combination of blocks that produce the same logic. The attached model contains an example of when this bug occurs in a MATLAB Function block.? ?

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Unexpected fixed-point data type propagation in MinMax block
Description: Reordering the inputs of the MinMax block may cause different output data types when both of these conditions exist:? The inputs of the?MinMax block are set to different fixed-point data types. The Output data type  parameter of the MinMax block is set to Inherit:Inherit via internal rule .?

Summary: The memory section associated with a subfunction is unpredictable
Description: Simulink Coder generates C subfunctions for the following modeling constructs:?    1)   Intrinsic math functions (e.g. lookup, fixpt)? 2) Noninlined Stateflow graphical functions? 3) Noninlined MATLAB subfunctions? When an Embedded target is chosen, the user can specify memory sections to be associated with the functions in the generated code. ?? These memory sections are used to associate pragmas with the functions.? In many cases, the memory section associated with the subfunctions is unpredictable. ?? Possibilities include:? 1) The Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Memory Sections > Shared Utility  memory section specified at the model level. 2) The Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Memory Sections > Execution  memory section specified at the model level. 3) The Configuration Parameters > Code Generation > Memory Sections > Execution  memory section specified for one of the subsystems. The determining factors  are difficult to predict, and are subject to bifurcation. ?? For example, adding a model block to a model can cause the shared utilities to switch from being dumped in the build directory to being dumped in the slprj/    _shareutils folder . ?

Summary: Protected model creation fails to locate configuration sets located in data dictionaries when the original model is using configset reference
Description: When trying to create a protected model while using a data dictionary, Simulink produces the following error message: Could not evaluate 'CodeGenerationConfigurationSet' in data dictionary Caused by: Error occurred.

Summary: Stateflow transitions are drawn on the wrong subviewer
Description: When you create a library containing a hierarchy of subcharted boxes (of depth greater than three levels) and supertransitions, it is possible?that when you save and reload the model, the transitions in the deepest part of the hierarchy are drawn on the wrong subviewer.

Summary: In MATLAB Function Block, using concatenation to initialize a dynamic matrix generates incorrect answers
Description: In MATLAB Function Block or MATLAB script, when you initialize a dynamic matrix, such as: y1 = [y1; [1 2 3 4] ];  the code generator generates incorrect dimensions that result in wrong answers. The concatenation used for initializing values into y1? generates incorrect dimension information. It also initializes y1  with incorrect values.  This bug?might occur when you write a horizontal concatenation expression that causes read-write issue of initializing memory for a dynamically allocated matrix.
Workaround: Quick workaround is to change the order of variables in the concatenation expression, such as:y1 = [ [1,2,3,4]; y1]

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Embedded Coder can produce unexpected code interface for a Simulink Function block connected to a root Outport block
Description: When you have a Simulink Function block connected to a root Outport block, unexpected code can be produced if all of the following conditions are met:  You are using Export-Function modeling style The signal dimensions of the root Outport block is greater than one The signals in the connection to the root Outport block cannot be uniquely identified with a single block (for example, if you use a Selector block or Demux block to perform partial selection of the signal; or, if you use Mux blocks and Demux blocks to rearrange the elements in the signal)
Workaround: Insert a Signal Conversion block immediately after any Mux, Demux, or Selector blocks on the signal path. ?By doing this, you are modifying your model so that the signal driving the root Outport block is uniquely identified with the Signal Conversion block. Select the Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization check box in the Signal Conversion block parameter dialog box.You can also automate the workaround process by using the script, Detect_1453109, provided in the attached ZIP file. To use the script, call with the model of interest, at the MATLAB command prompt: ?>> result = Detect_1453109('myModelName') ?If the model did not have any occurrence of the bug, then result will be 0. If the model does have the occurrence of the bug, then result will be 1, and a link to fix the bug will also be provided. Click on the fix link to open, and update the model to automatically apply the Signal Conversion block workaround described above.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect representation of down-counting array subscript with constant unsigned start and stop value
Description: MATLAB Coder might incorrectly represent a colon expression used to subscript an array when?the colon expression?satisfies all of the following conditions: The starting, increment, and ending values are compile-time constants The starting and ending values have an unsigned integer type The increment value is negative  For example, consider the?MATLAB function: function issue_1452753()A=[17    24     1     8    15;   23     5     7    14    16;    4     6    13    20    22;   10    12    19    21     3;   11    18    25     2     9]; temp = A(uint32(10):-1:uint32(2));showTemp(temp);end function showTemp(temp)coder.inline('never');disp(temp);end Executing?issue_1452753 ?in MATLAB produces the output:? 18 ? ?12 ? ? 6 ? ? 5 ? ?24 ? ?11 ? ?10 ? ? 4 ? ?23  If you generate a MEX file issue_1452753_mex?by using?MATLAB Coder and run it, you get an incorrect output:   18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18 ? ?18  See also Bug Report ID 1868474 .?
Workaround: Use a signed integer type for the starting and ending values of the colon expression.function issue_1452753()A=[17    24     1     8    15;       23     5     7    14    16;    4     6    13    20    22;   10    12    19    21     3;   11    18    25     2     9]; temp = A(int32(10):-1:int32(2));end function showTemp(temp)coder.inline('never');disp(temp);end

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible incorrect code generation for nested handle objects with different lifetimes
Description: Code generation may generate incorrect code for MATLAB code with all of these conditions: An outer object that contains another handle object as its property. The inner object is assigned inside the constructor of the outer object. The lifetime of the inner object is greater than the lifetime of the outer object (there is a function whose caller(s) can access the inner object but not the outer object).  For example, the following MATLAB program meets these conditions and generates incorrect code: function y = foo(n)    o = sub(n);        y = o.p;endfunction o = sub(n)    coder.inline('never')    obj = MyOuter(n);    o = obj.p;end? classdef MyOuter < handle    properties        p;    end    methods        function o = MyOuter(n)            o.p = MyInner(n);        end    end?? end  In this program, the inner object can be accessed in function foo  because it is returned from function sub , but the lifetime of the outer object is limited only to the function sub .

For example, in some circumstances, the model _data.c  file can contain only comments.  The simulation produces an error message that includes this text: Instrumented source %s must have a line of form #define qqqbranches n

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: AUTOSAR model with implicit read inside Stateflow function generates incorrect RTE API name
Description: AUTOSAR code generation generates an incorrect RTE API name if both  of the following conditions are true: A root inport configured with AUTOSAR implicit read access directly feeds a Stateflow chart. In the chart, a Stateflow function accesses the root inport.  Under these conditions, the generated Rte_IRead  API name contains an incorrect runnable name.
Workaround: To work around this bug, add a Signal Conversion block between the root inport and the Stateflow chart.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Generated code for Stateflow Chart may be incorrect when a variable is used in multiple parallel states
Description: The generated code may be incorrect for a Stateflow Chart that meets these conditions: The Stateflow Chart contains multiple parallel states. At least two of the parallel states use the same variable. For the states that use the variable, the Function Inline Option  parameter is set to Auto  or Function . The variable is used in an entry action, during action, and transition.  The code generator may incorrectly localize the variable and eliminate its assignment statement.
Workaround: You can workaround this issue by implementing one of the following options:1. For the parallel states, set the Function Inline Option parameter to Inline.or2. Run the attached p script to disable the enhanced localization optimization. Note, by disabling this optimization, other variables in your model may not be localized resulting in increased RAM and ROM consumption.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code replacement?of operations with?conceptual arguments of type pointer can generate incorrect code
Description: Code replacement of operations with conceptual arguments of type pointer can generate incorrect code when all of the following conditions are true: Code generation uses a custom code replacement library that replaces an operation with conceptual arguments of type pointer.  The code replacement entry specifies an implementation argument with a base type that is not equal to the base type of the data passed to the replacement function.   For example, a code replacement entry matches the following memcpy  operation: int16_T data_in[n];int16_T data_out[n];memcpy(&data_out[0], &data_in[0], n*sizeof(int16_T));  The implementation representation in the code replacement entry has the following prototype: void* my_custom_memcpy(uint8_T*, uint8_T*, size_t);  In this case, the code generator generates the following incorrect code: uint8_T tmp_in = (uint8_T) data_in[0];uint8_T tmp_out = (uint8_T) data_out[0];my_custom_memcpy(&tmp_out, &tmp_in,  n*sizeof(int16_T));

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Using an undefined variable as an index into a matrix or cell array might produce an incorrect answer
Description: When indexing into a matix or cell array with an expression that uses a variable that is not defined, the result in generated code might be different from the result in MATLAB? . Consider the following function: function res=foo(in1) if in1 > 0     x = 2; end if in1 == 0     x = 3; end a = 1:5; res = a(x);   If  in1  is less than zero, x  is not defined. MATLAB produces this error message:  Undefined function or variable 'x'.   When the code generation products, such as MATLAB?  Coder? and Simulink?  Coder?,  detect that an undefined variable is used in an expression that indexes into a matrix or cell array, they produce an error message like: ??? Variable 'x' is not fully defined on some execution paths  However, in some cases, the code generator fails to detect this situation. Instead,  it assigns the variable an arbitrary value in the generated code. This bug occurs only when the original MATLAB code fails to assign a variable before using it in an expression  that  indexes into a matrix or cell array. In these cases, the code fails in MATLAB.
Workaround: Define all variables used in an expression that indexes into a matrix or  cell array. For the previous example, define x on all execution paths that lead to res = a(x). function res=foo(in1) x = 1; % ADDED TO RESOLVE THE BUGif in1 > 0     x = 2; end if in1 == 0     x = 3; end a = 1:5; res = a(x);

Description: Incorrect rounding behavior?can occur in the generated code when casting an integral value (with type double, single, fixed-point, or integer) to a?fixed-point type with non power-of-2 scaling. This issue can occur for casts in your MATLAB code or casts that occur during code generation.? Run the MEX function. >>foo_mex(1)  ans =?  ? ? ? ? ? ? 1073741825.281  ? ? ? ? ? DataTypeMode: Fixed-point: slope and bias scaling  ? ? ? ? ? ? Signedness: Signed  ? ? ? ? ? ? WordLength: 62  ? ? ? ? ? ? ? ? ?Slope: 2.3846  ? ? ? ? ? ? ? ? ? Bias: 0
